serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Daun Kesum is approximately 5 grams (a small handful of fresh leaves), as used for garnishing a bowl of Laksa or as part of an ulam herb platter.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Coriander (cilantro) leaves, raw (NDB 02013) used as closest approved-source analog for fresh aromatic herb; supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian herbs
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No dedicated USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Persicaria odorata (Vietnamese coriander / Daun Kesum). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fresh herb entries (cilantro/coriander leaves, raw; FDC ID 169997) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional herb composition data. The input record's values (energy 35 kcal, protein 2.5 g, fat 0.5 g, carbohydrate 6.2 g per 100 g) are consistent with the range reported for fresh aromatic herbs in these approved sources and are retained with medium confidence. Vitamin and mineral values are similarly consistent with approved-source herb profiles.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Leafy Herbs — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate fresh herb category; reference: University of Sydney GI Database category guidance for non-starchy vegetables and herbs
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test exists for Persicaria odorata (Daun Kesum) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 5 (low) based on the established principle that non-starchy leafy herbs with negligible available carbohydrate content have a negligible glycemic response. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance for non-starchy vegetables. Glycemic load per 5 g serving = (5 × 6.2 × 0.05) / 100 ≈ 0.02, effectively negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g) and high potassium (450 mg/100 g) content
hypertension.guidance: Daun Kesum is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content and substantial potassium level support healthy blood pressure regulation, and it can be freely used as a flavoring herb or in ulam without sodium concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low available carbohydrate (6.2 g/100 g), negligible glycemic index (~5), and high dietary fibre (3.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Daun Kesum is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; its negligible glycemic index and low carbohydrate content mean it contributes virtually no glycemic load, while its dietary fibre may support postprandial glucose moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and antioxidant phytochemicals
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Daun Kesum poses no cardiovascular concern; its near-zero saturated fat and cholesterol content, combined with potassium and antioxidant compounds, make it a heart-friendly herb suitable for regular inclusion in a cardiovascular-protective diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Phytochemicals and antioxidants with potential anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Daun Kesum contains phytochemicals including flavonoids and essential oils that have demonstrated antioxidant activity in vitro; while clinical evidence in humans is limited, its inclusion as a culinary herb is unlikely to exacerbate inflammation and may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Daun Kesum (Persicaria odorata), commonly known as Vietnamese coriander, laksa leaf, or polygonum, is a pungent aromatic herb native to Southeast Asia and widely used in Malay, Vietnamese, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines. The lance-shaped leaves are easily identified by a distinctive dark reddish-purple chevron marking at the center. The herb delivers a bold, lemony, slightly peppery, and mildly astringent flavor that is more intense than common coriander. It is an irreplaceable ingredient in Laksa (spiced coconut noodle soup) and Asam Pedas (sour-spicy fish stew), where it provides aromatic depth that balances rich, spicy, and sour flavor profiles. It is also eaten raw as part of ulam, the traditional Malay herb salad served alongside rice and sambal. Nutritionally, Daun Kesum is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate herb providing approximately 35 kcal per 100 g, with 2.5 g protein, 0.5 g fat, and 6.2 g carbohydrate. It is a notable source of vitamin A (450 mcg RAE), vitamin K (150 mcg), potassium (450 mg), calcium (120 mg), and iron (2.5 mg) per 100 g, making it nutritionally significant despite being consumed in small quantities. Its negligible sodium content (5 mg/100 g) and high potassium make it suitable for hypertension management. The glycemic index is negligible (estimated ~5), making it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes dietary plans. It is fully vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

The input values are consistent with approved-source herb profiles and are retained at medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database category guidance for non-starchy herbs.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Persicaria odorata is not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA database as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific herb is not reported in the approved sources consulted. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based food, naturally contains no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — fresh leafy herb, negligible vitamin D as expected for non-animal, non-fortified plant food). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (naturally zero — unprocessed fresh herb). Saturated fat estimated at 0.05 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central fresh herb analogy (cilantro/coriander leaves). Ontology codes are approximate anchors only; no dedicated FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for Persicaria odorata specifically. GI value of 5 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI test for Daun Kesum was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
